Households in Capricorn District
Households experiencing severe drought within last 5 years
Households reporting crop failure
Households reporting water scarcity
Households reporting livestock mortality
Households food insecure in Mopani District
Households food insecure in Capricorn District
Households practicing rainwater harvesting
Households practicing crop diversification

qual_findings.quote: "Key enablers of resilience included strong social networks, traditional and indigenous knowledge, and institutional support, though limited agricultural extension services constrained adaptation capacity."
qual_findings.finding: Resilience is enabled by social capital, indigenous knowledge, and institutions—but extension services are failing, creating a critical gap.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-025 (social networks) and SR-026 (governance)

qual_findings.quote: "Female-headed households experienced greater food shortages and faced barriers to livestock destocking."
qual_findings.finding: Gender inequality intersects with adaptation: women face both greater impacts and specific barriers to implementing coping strategies.
qual_findings.notes: Direct evidence for Paper 2

qual_findings.quote: "The study advocates for gender-responsive, equity-oriented drought policies, strengthened extension systems, and the integration of local knowledge to enhance resilience among vulnerable rural communities."
qual_findings.finding: Policy recommendations directly address the barriers identified: gender-responsive policy, fixing extension, and valuing indigenous knowledge.
qual_findings.notes: Actionable policy pathway

qual_findings.quote: "Low-income households were more affected by drought impacts overall."
qual_findings.finding: Poverty amplifies vulnerability to climate shocks—those with least resources suffer most.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-031 findings

papers.citation: An Assessment of Water Accessibility in the Kuvukiland Informal Settlement of Tsumeb in Namibia
papers.title: An Assessment of Water Accessibility in the Kuvukiland Informal Settlement of Tsumeb in Namibia

quant_findings.metric: % collecting water daily
% collecting water weekly
% collecting water monthly
% participating through public debate
% participating through organized committee meetings

qual_findings.quote: "We supply water to Kuvukiland informal settlement through a bulk water supply, which works like a pre-paid system" (Interviewee 5).
qual_findings.finding: Prepaid water system is the formal mechanism, but it fails in practice.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-032 findings on metering destroying social networks

qual_findings.quote: "We as the community dig the trenches ourselves; what the municipality does is supply the water pipes and erect the standpipes for us. However, the standpipes are not reliable because they do not work in line with the card system; they overcharge us" (Interviewee 3).
qual_findings.finding: Community provides labor (sweat equity), but infrastructure fails and overcharges—double burden.
qual_findings.notes: Parallels Botswana ungazetted settlements' self-provision

qual_findings.quote: "During rainy seasons, residents harvest water from the roof of their iron zinc houses" (Interviewee 3).
qual_findings.finding: Rainwater harvesting as autonomous adaptation—identical to Botswana RWH practices.
qual_findings.notes: Directly comparable to SR-018, SR-031, SR-032

qual_findings.quote: "Sometimes when the water situation reaches a critical point, they send sprinkler trucks filled with water, which is always brownish in colour. Water supply is very poor here and the municipality does not really look after the water in Kuvukiland" (Interviewee 3).
qual_findings.finding: Emergency water is poor quality; institutional neglect is explicit.
qual_findings.notes: Aligns with SR-027 (quality complaints) and SR-026 (institutional failure)

qual_findings.quote: "Water is rarely available here; we sleep standing in queues at the water collection points. Most of the time we collect water from public toilets in Soweto" (Interviewee 3).
qual_findings.finding: Extreme coping: queuing overnight; resorting to public toilets as water source—sanitation infrastructure repurposed for water.
qual_findings.notes: Extreme manifestation of alt_source reliance

qual_findings.quote: "The standpipes are far from our houses, they are not spread out into the community, we want them to extend the standpipes, because the distance is too much for us" (Interviewee 6).
qual_findings.finding: Distance as primary barrier—echoes SR-001-Q11 (distance problem).
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-020 (donkey carts) and SR-001

qual_findings.quote: "Soweto location is very far for us and sometimes we just go without water, because we cannot afford to travel the long distance every day, especially for us women" (Interviewee 5).
qual_findings.finding: Gendered burden: women bear the cost of distance; sometimes give up entirely.
qual_findings.notes: Direct parallel to SR-006, SR-032 (gendered fetching burden)

qual_findings.quote: "Our kids get infected by the water they bring by trucks, and they usually suffer from diarrhoea" (Interviewee 3).
qual_findings.finding: Health impact of poor-quality emergency water—children most affected.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-010 (diarrhea prevalence)

qual_findings.quote: "Buying the pre-paid [water] is a huge challenge; we cannot afford the N$180.00 paid for the card" (Interviewee 7).
qual_findings.finding: Economic barrier: upfront cost of prepaid system excludes the poor.
qual_findings.notes: Parallels SR-009 (92% overburdened) and SR-023 (willing but unable)

qual_findings.quote: "Even if we, the community leaders, attend meetings on behalf of the community to air our views, people only listen but they do nothing about it" (Interviewee 5).
qual_findings.finding: Tokenistic participation—echoes SR-026 (governance failures) and SR-007 (low institutional trust).
qual_findings.notes: Direct parallel to SR-026's findings on participatory governance

qual_findings.quote: "When it comes to the issue of water, decisions with regard to water are very poor and they are less integrated, because we hardly see our views visible in the planning process. We need a co-ordinated effort; one that fits all parties" (Interviewee 3).
qual_findings.finding: Institutional failure: planning excludes community; call for integration.
qual_findings.notes: Aligns with Paper 2 (institutional friction)

qual_findings.quote: "We have challenges of water supply to Kuvukiland because the town is not formalised, so we are not supposed to supply water to the informal settlement. We also work in accordance with the Local Authority Act and, according to the Act, we are supposed to supply water to every residence" (Interviewee 2).
qual_findings.finding: Policy contradiction: informal settlement status vs. universal service obligation. Central friction point.
qual_findings.notes: Core finding: directly parallels Botswana's gazetted/ungazetted policy exclusion

papers.citation: Center for African Studies, University of Florida (2022). Challenges Facing Community Management of Rural Water Supply: The Case of Ohangwena Region, Namibia.
papers.title: Challenges Facing Community Management of Rural Water Supply: The Case of Ohangwena Region, Namibia

qual_findings.quote: "At least half of the existing water points in rural areas in Namibia are faulty and dysfunctional, however, and the majority of people are still struggling to access clean water."
qual_findings.finding: Despite policy commitment to community management, infrastructure failure is rampant—undermining the entire model.
qual_findings.notes: Aligns with SR-027 (broken standpipes) and SR-026 (institutional failure)

qual_findings.quote: "Rural water supply in most developing countries have been shaped by mismanagement. The resultant effect of this mismanagement is increased poverty, tremendous human health costs, as well as gender and other social inequalities."
qual_findings.finding: Links water mismanagement directly to poverty, health, and inequality—framing water as a social justice issue, not just a technical one.
qual_findings.notes: Provides framing for your thesis's normative argument

qual_findings.quote: "Over 50 percent of Namibians live in rural areas, and many live far from available water sources."
qual_findings.finding: Geographic distance remains a fundamental barrier despite policy reforms.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-001-Q11 (distance) and SR-020 (donkey carts)

qual_findings.quote: "Water supply in rural areas is faced by several challenges, such as poor economic conditions, location of settlements in environmentally fragile areas, management models dominated by diverse cultural values, and associated cost recovery challenges."
qual_findings.finding: Multidimensional barriers: economic, environmental, cultural, financial—all interact.
qual_findings.notes: Supports STF framework's multi-level approach

qual_findings.quote: "Scattered settlements bring about challenges for infrastructure provision. Oftentimes, the condition of infrastructure systems becomes poor due to technical, financial and managerial limitations. As a result, the rural population depends on local water sources (wells, hand pumps, river), which are frequently contaminated."
qual_findings.finding: Settlement pattern (scattered vs. concentrated) directly affects infrastructure viability. Direct parallel to Botswana's gazetted/ungazetted distinction.
qual_findings.notes: Key comparative finding—parallels Botswana settlement policy debate

qual_findings.quote: "The community management model in post-independence Namibia came into prominence in 1998. The literature links it to the Decentralization Enabling Act of 2000 and National Water Policy 1998."
qual_findings.finding: Policy timeline establishes when community management became official doctrine.
qual_findings.notes: Useful for historical framing

qual_findings.quote: "The sustainability of the community management model depends on technical, managerial and financial aspects to ensure the long-term sustainability and functionality, as well as the participation of users."
qual_findings.finding: Identifies three pillars for success—technical, managerial, financial—plus participation.
qual_findings.notes: Directly comparable to Paper 1's CSFs

qual_findings.quote: "Community management empowers and prepares the community members to undertake a leading role in planning, construction, financing, and managing water supply systems necessary for water sustainability after the implementing agency/government has left the community."
qual_findings.finding: Theory of community management: empowerment leads to sustainability
qual_findings.notes: The ideal against which reality is measured

qual_findings.quote: "This management model has some limitations, including: lack of institutional support, inadequate financial resources, weak tariff schemes, maintenance issues or difficulty in obtaining spare parts, and lack of support from external entities."
qual_findings.finding: Explicit list of barriers to community management success.
qual_findings.notes: Directly comparable to Paper 1's systemic challenges

qual_findings.quote: "Governance, leadership attributes of the committee members, training and capacity building, level of community involvement, coordination and support are critical success factors for effective management of rural water supplies."
qual_findings.finding: CSFs identified: governance, leadership, training, participation, coordination.
qual_findings.notes: Parallels Paper 1's CSF findings—strengthens cross-country comparison

papers.citation: African Journal of Environmental Sciences & Renewable Energy (2025). Evaluating the Effectiveness of Sustainable Water Management Practices in Promoting Community Health in Kavango East Region of Namibia.
papers.title: Evaluating the Effectiveness of Sustainable Water Management Practices in Promoting Community Health in Kavango East Region of Namibia

qual_findings.quote: "The Namibian Integrated Rural Water Supply and Sanitation Policy of 2008 aims to ensure that at least 80% of rural households have access to potable water within 20 minutes of their homes, unfortunately currently, only 42% of rural communities in Namibia have this access."
qual_findings.finding: A stark policy implementation gap: national target (80%) vs. current reality (42%) for rural water access. This quantified gap is a form of institutional friction.
qual_findings.notes: Provides a powerful national statistic (42%) to frame the problem.

qual_findings.quote: "Waterborne diseases account for 30% of all outpatient visits in rural health facilities."
qual_findings.finding: Health systems are overwhelmed by preventable waterborne illness—direct link between water insecurity and public health burden.
qual_findings.notes: Quantifiable health impact (30%)—can be used in intro or discussion.

qual_findings.quote: "Coping with the consequences of this polluted water while living in a persistently water-scarce environment imposes what Shikangala and Haimbili (2022) estimate as a 15% toll on the annual income of the average rural Namibian family—expenses and lost productivity."
qual_findings.finding: Economic friction: households lose 15% of annual income to coping with water insecurity—money that could otherwise support development.
qual_findings.notes: Directly comparable to SR-027-Q16 (avoidance costs) and SR-009 (affordability burden).

qual_findings.quote: "These places harvested water because they have facilities." While some isolated homesteads also participate in this practice, many community members lack the means to do so due to their housing structures.
qual_findings.finding: Infrastructure inequality: water harvesting is only possible where facilities exist—excludes those with inadequate housing.
qual_findings.notes: Parallels Botswana's gazetted/ungazetted divide.

qual_findings.quote: "covering well reduced evaporation and water lasted until the next rain."
qual_findings.finding: Indigenous technical knowledge: simple, effective conservation practiced by communities.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-030 (indigenous practices) and SR-018 (traditional RWH).

qual_findings.quote: "areas where there are dense forests receive good rainfall"
qual_findings.finding: Local ecological knowledge: communities understand forest-water linkages—basis for tree planting as adaptation.
qual_findings.notes: Unique finding—adds dimension not常见 in other studies.

qual_findings.quote: "Community members (men) put roots of a certain plant to kill fish."
qual_findings.finding: Maladaptation: traditional practices (fish poisoning) threaten water quality—highlights need for education, not just technology.
qual_findings.notes: Nuanced finding—not all traditional practices are beneficial.

qual_findings.quote: "The availability of piped water in some communities enhances equitable access to water. This creates equality" (CM 4).
qual_findings.finding: Piped water as equity mechanism—when present, it reduces inequality.
qual_findings.notes: Positive case—shows what works when infrastructure functions.

qual_findings.quote: "The obvious benefit of sustainable water management is improved health. The community has access to clean water" (CM 11).
qual_findings.finding: Direct link between water management and health outcomes—community recognizes the connection.
qual_findings.notes: Complements QL2—community awareness of health link.

qual_findings.quote: "Taking charge of water sources by the community develops responsibility and empowerment" (OF 1).
qual_findings.finding: Community management as empowerment—the theory behind CBNRM
qual_findings.notes: The ideal against which failure is measured.

qual_findings.quote: "Water is conveniently available and it creates more time to do other commitments, hence enhancing community development" (OF 3).
qual_findings.finding: Time poverty reduction: when water is available, time is freed for other development activities.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-001-Q11 (distance) and SR-020-Q3 (donkey carts)—shows positive opposite.

qual_findings.quote: "Both directing running water into fields and drip irrigation has impact on group growth. This ensures high crop productivity" (CM 12).
qual_findings.finding: Irrigation as productivity enhancement—economic benefit of water management.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-030-Q4 (irrigation adoption).

qual_findings.quote: "Sometimes it takes more than two months for the responsible team to report for repairs" (CM 6).
qual_findings.finding: Institutional failure: delayed repairs leave communities without water for months.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-027 (broken standpipes) and SR-026 (institutional failure).

qual_findings.quote: "Sometimes the community leaders sit on the information, or do not report to the grassroots level" (CM 7).
qual_findings.finding: Information hierarchy failure: knowledge stops at leadership, doesn't reach those who need it.
qual_findings.notes: Parallels SR-007 (low institutional trust) and SR-026 (participatory governance failure).

qual_findings.quote: "meetings on sustainable water management are mostly held at constituency or regional level" (CM 13).
qual_findings.finding: Participatory governance failure: meetings happen above community level—grassroots excluded.
qual_findings.notes: Echoes SR-026 (tokenistic participation) and SR-007 (trust gap).

qual_findings.quote: "a Protect source of domestic water ensure access to clean water and promotes health" (CM 8).
qual_findings.finding: Protected sources work—when they exist, health improves.
qual_findings.notes: Positive case—what works.

qual_findings.quote: "The majority of the people who live along the Kavango River still use water from Kavango... There is no quality" (CM 6).
qual_findings.finding: River water reliance persists despite risks—lack of alternatives.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-020, SR-021 (river dependence).

qual_findings.quote: families rely on open wells, leading to reported cases of waterborne diseases.
qual_findings.finding: Open wells = health risk. Direct link between source type and health outcomes.
qual_findings.notes: Parallels SR-010 (diarrhea prevalence).

qual_findings.quote: "development and provision of better water services follow institutions"
qual_findings.finding: Institutional bias: water services follow institutions (schools, clinics), not people. Communities are left out.
qual_findings.notes: Core finding: parallels Botswana's gazetted/ungazetted policy exclusion.

qual_findings.quote: "some of the community members are conservative" (OF 4), particularly the elderly, who view traditional practices, like using water from open sources, as safe.
qual_findings.finding: Cultural barrier: traditional beliefs about water safety persist, hindering adoption of improved sources.
qual_findings.notes: Nuanced finding—not just infrastructure, but beliefs.

qual_findings.quote: "practical demonstrations would convince the community members" (OF 4).
qual_findings.finding: Demonstration as effective extension—showing, not just telling.
qual_findings.notes: Policy-relevant: how to change behavior.

qual_findings.quote: "The tradition of abandoning projects makes the community lose confidence" (CM 6).
qual_findings.finding: Project abandonment destroys trust—once burned, twice shy.
qual_findings.notes: Complements SR-007 (low institutional trust).
